preamble: The voiceless postalveolar fricative (ʃ) underwent a significant historical sound change in Spanish, backing to become the voiceless uvular fricative (χ). This transformation was a key part of the broader "readjustment of the sibilants" in the language, which aimed to resolve a high functional load on similar-sounding sibilants.

examples.note: This illustrates the shift from Old Spanish /ʃ/ (represented by 'x' in orthography, as in 'oxo') to Modern Spanish /χ/ (represented by 'j', as in 'ojo') during the 16th and 17th centuries.
The 'x' in the name 'México' originally represented the /ʃ/ sound, which later shifted to /χ/ in most Spanish dialects.

sources: Lapesa, R. (1981). Historia de la lengua española. Gredos.
